title: Docker Compose publishes backend service ports and bypasses the gateway
description:
docker-compose.yml publishes auth-service through activity-service directly on host ports 5001-5006. Because the gateway is supposed to be the only public entry point, these port mappings let callers bypass gateway authentication, rate limiting, X-User-Id injection, and internal-route blocking entirely. Root cause: every backend service uses `ports:` instead of internal-only networking (`expose:` or no host publishing). This already exposes each service's /health and /openapi endpoints; once real endpoints land it will also expose protected and internal routes. Fix by removing host port publishing for internal services and keeping only api-gateway public by default, with opt-in debug exposure only when explicitly needed.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the stack with `docker compose up`.
2. Call a backend service directly on a published host port such as `http://localhost:5001/health`.
3. Observe the request reaches the service without passing through the gateway protections.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Only the API Gateway is published to the host by default.
- [ ] Backend services remain reachable only on the internal Docker network unless an explicit debug override is enabled.
- [ ] Docker Compose/docs/tests are updated so the intended exposure model is clear and verifiable.
acceptance_criteria:
Only the API Gateway is published to the host by default, and backend service ports are internal unless an explicit debug override is enabled.
Compose/docs/smoke checks make the intended exposure model obvious and verifiable, including proof that direct host access to internal services fails by default while the gateway remains reachable.
Direct host access to internal services is treated as a security-boundary regression that must stay closed before demos or broader external testing.

title: Complex milestone types
description:
Add advanced milestone types beyond the default simple consistency target to the Challenge experience.

## Extension Model

title: Host-run Development settings point services at the wrong Postgres ports
description:
Host-run development currently points services at the wrong PostgreSQL ports. Decide and document the supported local-development topology, then align the Development connection settings with that choice.

## Scope
- [ ] Decide whether host-run services against compose-managed databases are a supported workflow.
- [ ] If supported, fix each service's Development connection string/port mapping so it targets the intended database container.
- [ ] If not supported, document the supported alternative clearly enough that contributors do not lose time chasing a broken topology.
- [ ] Add a lightweight validation path so local developers can confirm they are connected to the right database instance.

## Notes
- This issue directly affects local implementation/debugging speed.
- Avoid a half-supported setup where some services use container defaults and others require manual rewrites.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the compose databases locally.
2. Run a backend service on the host in Development mode.
3. Observe whether it connects to the intended database instance without manual rewrites.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The supported local-development topology is explicit and works without surprise port mismatches.
Host-run services either connect to the correct compose databases or the unsupported path is clearly documented away.
Developers have a simple validation path, including a reproducible host-run/compose-managed smoke check or equivalent documented verification, to confirm they are talking to the intended database.

description:

## Non-Negotiables

## Before Finishing Checklist

- [ ] Backend (per service): dotnet format --verify-no-changes, dotnet build, dotnet test

title: Gateway still boots with a checked-in fallback JWT signing secret
description:
`services/gateway/src/Program.cs` reads `Jwt:Secret` from configuration, and earlier work already made environment-based secret injection possible. The remaining security bug is that the gateway still boots with a checked-in fallback value from `services/gateway/src/appsettings.json` (`CHANGE-THIS-IN-PRODUCTION-minimum-32-characters-long`). That means the gateway does not fail closed when an environment forgets to provide a real secret; it starts successfully with a publicly known signing key.

This issue should now be treated as fail-closed startup validation plus removal of the checked-in fallback, not as generic secret configuration work.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the gateway without setting `Jwt__Secret`.
2. Observe startup succeeds because appsettings.json provides the placeholder string.
3. Tokens signed with that known value are accepted by the gateway.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The gateway refuses to start unless a non-placeholder signing secret is provided externally.
Checked-in configuration no longer contains a usable JWT secret fallback.
Tests prove missing or placeholder secrets fail startup in the relevant hosting paths (for example local test host and compose/dev startup validation), and the startup failure is explicit enough to diagnose configuration mistakes quickly.

title: JetStream streams are never created — all NATS publish/subscribe will fail at runtime
description:
NatsEventPublisher uses JetStream (NatsJSContext.PublishAsync) and NatsEventSubscriber uses JetStream consumers (CreateOrUpdateConsumerAsync + ConsumeAsync). Both require JetStream streams to exist for the subjects defined in Winzy.Contracts.Subjects. However: (1) NatsServiceExtensions.AddNatsMessaging only registers the NATS connection and publisher — no stream creation. (2) docker-compose.yml NATS service uses --jetstream flag but mounts no config file defining streams. (3) No service startup code creates streams. Result: when any service tries to publish or subscribe to a NATS event, it will fail with no responders or stream not found. Fix: either create streams in NatsServiceExtensions during DI setup, add a startup HostedService that ensures streams exist, or add a NATS config file to docker-compose that pre-creates streams.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a clean environment with NATS JetStream enabled.
2. Publish or subscribe using `NatsEventPublisher`/`NatsEventSubscriber` without manually creating streams.
3. Observe publish or consumer startup fails because the required JetStream streams do not exist.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Required JetStream streams are created automatically in a clean environment.
- [ ] Publish and subscribe flows succeed without manual stream bootstrapping.
- [ ] Automated tests or startup validation cover the stream setup path.

title: global.json SDK pinning with latestPatch will break Docker builds and CI when new feature band releases
description:
global.json pins SDK version to 10.0.102 with rollForward: latestPatch. The latestPatch policy only accepts SDKs in the same feature band (10.0.1xx). Docker images (mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:10.0-alpine) and CI (setup-dotnet with 10.0.x) install the latest available 10.0.xxx SDK. When .NET releases a new feature band (e.g. 10.0.200), Docker builds and CI will fail because the SDK 10.0.2xx does not match the required feature band 10.0.1xx. Fix options: (1) change rollForward to latestFeature to accept any 10.0.xxx, (2) pin the Docker image tag to a specific SDK version matching global.json, or (3) use latestMinor for maximum flexibility.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Use a newer .NET 10 feature-band SDK than `10.0.1xx` (for example `10.0.2xx`).
2. Run the current build flow or Docker build with the checked-in `global.json`.
3. Observe the SDK selection fails because `latestPatch` does not roll forward across feature bands.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The pinned SDK strategy works for local builds, Docker, and CI as new .NET 10 feature bands ship.
- [ ] The repository no longer depends on a stale feature-band match to build successfully.
- [ ] The chosen SDK policy is documented or encoded consistently across tooling.

title: fix: lint-staged ESLint config not found from repo root
description: lint-staged runs `frontend/node_modules/.bin/eslint --no-warn-ignored` from the repo root, but ESLint v9 looks for `eslint.config.*` starting from cwd (the repo root). The config lives at `frontend/eslint.config.mjs`, so ESLint fails on every frontend commit with 'couldn't find an eslint.config' error. Fix: add `--config frontend/eslint.config.mjs` to the lint-staged command in the root `package.json`.

title: Gateway rate limiting partitions by RemoteIpAddress — breaks behind reverse proxy
description:
In services/gateway/src/Program.cs lines 41 and 50, rate limiting uses context.Connection.RemoteIpAddress as the partition key. When the Gateway runs behind any reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, cloud load balancer), RemoteIpAddress will be the proxy IP, not the client IP. All clients then share one rate limit bucket (100 req/min for default, 10 req/min for auth), effectively rate-limiting ALL users collectively. The pipeline also lacks UseForwardedHeaders() middleware, so X-Forwarded-For headers are not processed. Fix: (1) add builder.Services.Configure<ForwardedHeadersOptions>() and app.UseForwardedHeaders() before UseRateLimiter(), (2) use the forwarded client IP for rate limit partitioning.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run the gateway behind a reverse proxy or load balancer.
2. Send requests from multiple clients through that proxy.
3. Observe rate limiting keys off the proxy IP instead of the real client IP, causing clients to share a bucket.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Forwarded headers are processed before rate limiting.
- [ ] Rate limiting partitions requests by the real client identity when proxied.
- [ ] Tests cover proxied request behavior so the fix is durable.

title: NatsEventSubscriber: null-forgiving operator on msg.Data risks NullReferenceException
description:
In shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs line 25: await HandleAsync(msg.Data!, stoppingToken) — the ! null-forgiving operator suppresses the nullable warning but does NOT prevent null at runtime. If a NATS message has a null or unparseable payload, NatsJsonSerializer returns null for reference types, and the ! operator passes null directly to HandleAsync which expects non-null T. This causes NullReferenceException, which (combined with the missing error handling in the consume loop) permanently kills the subscriber. Fix: add a null check before calling HandleAsync, and either skip the message with a warning log or nack/dead-letter it.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Deliver a null or unparseable payload to `NatsEventSubscriber`.
2. Let the subscriber deserialize and call `HandleAsync(msg.Data!)`.
3. Observe a null payload can trigger a runtime failure in the consumer loop.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Null or invalid payloads are handled safely before `HandleAsync` is invoked.
- [ ] The consumer loop stays alive after malformed messages.
- [ ] Tests cover the null/invalid payload path.

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Should be fixed alongside winzy.ai-2uz (subscriber error handling) since both are changes to the same function in NatsEventSubscriber.cs. The null check (this issue) is a subset of proper error handling (2uz). Fixing both together in one PR makes the most sense.

title: Gateway trusts forwarded headers from any client and makes rate limits spoofable
description:
`services/gateway/src/Program.cs` enables `UseForwardedHeaders()` and then clears both `KnownIPNetworks` and `KnownProxies`, which makes ASP.NET accept `X-Forwarded-For` and `X-Forwarded-Proto` from any remote peer. Because the rate limiter keys on `context.Connection.RemoteIpAddress`, an internet client can send a forged `X-Forwarded-For` header and choose its own rate-limit bucket.

This is effectively a regression from the earlier reverse-proxy/rate-limiting fix: proxy-aware behavior was added, but the trust boundary was widened too far. The plan should explicitly capture that only configured reverse proxies/load balancers are allowed to supply forwarding headers.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run the gateway directly or behind an untrusted hop.
2. Send repeated requests with different forged `X-Forwarded-For` values.
3. Observe the effective client IP / rate-limit partition changes even though the real peer is the same client.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Forwarded headers are honored only from explicitly configured proxy IPs/networks.
Direct clients cannot influence the resolved client IP via spoofed forwarding headers.
Tests cover trusted and untrusted proxy scenarios, including integration-style verification of rate-limit partitioning and forwarded-header handling, and docs/examples show the expected trusted-proxy configuration.

title: Service health endpoints report healthy even when DB or NATS are down
description:
All backend services currently register only bare `AddHealthChecks()` and map `/health` without real dependency probes. That means `/health` can return success while PostgreSQL or NATS is unavailable, and the gateway can aggregate those false positives into a misleading green platform status.

Fix this by wiring real dependency health checks and making failures diagnosable, not merely non-200.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a backend service with PostgreSQL or NATS unavailable.
2. Call that service's `/health` endpoint.
3. Observe it still reports healthy even though a required dependency is down.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Each backend service health endpoint reports DB and NATS connectivity, not just process liveness.
Tests cover per-service health payload/status when DB is down, when NATS is down, and when the gateway aggregates one unhealthy downstream service, with at least one realistic integration/smoke path for the aggregated result.
Dependency probe failures emit useful logs so incidents and failing tests can be diagnosed quickly.

title: Notification Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Notification Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] GET /notifications — Own notifications (paginated)
- [ ] PUT /notifications/{id}/read — Mark as read
- [ ] PUT /notifications/read-all — Mark all as read
- [ ] GET /notifications/unread-count — Unread count
- [ ] PUT /notifications/settings — Update preferences

## Notification Record Model (notification_db)
- notifications table: id, user_id, type, data (jsonb), read_at, created_at
- notification_settings table: user_id, habit_reminders (bool), friend_activity (bool), challenge_updates (bool)
- Backend stores structured notification type/data; frontend surfaces own the final user-facing copy and tone.

## NATS Subscriptions
- [ ] habit.completed → create structured friend-activity notification when the recipient/settings allow it
- [ ] friend.request.sent → create structured friend-request notification
- [ ] friend.request.accepted → create structured request-accepted notification
- [ ] challenge.created → create structured challenge invitation/update notification
- [ ] challenge.completed → create structured challenge-completed notification
- [ ] user.deleted → delete notifications and settings for userId

## Boundaries And Tone
- [ ] Keep notification semantics supportive and non-punishing, but do not move final display strings into the backend.
- [ ] Store enough typed data that frontend surfaces can render warm copy consistently.

## Notes
- Push notification delivery is tracked separately in winzy.ai-bay.
- Start with in-app notification records stored in DB.

title: Gateway JWT secret not configurable via environment variable in docker-compose.yml
description:
In docker-compose.yml, the api-gateway service has no Jwt__Secret environment variable override. The JWT secret is hardcoded only in services/gateway/src/appsettings.json as a placeholder string. This is inconsistent with how all other config (ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection, Nats__Url) is externalized via environment variables in docker-compose.yml for the downstream services. Fix: add Jwt__Secret environment variable to the api-gateway service in docker-compose.yml, matching the pattern used for DB/NATS config.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Inspect the `api-gateway` environment in `docker-compose.yml`.
2. Compare it with the gateway JWT configuration.
3. Observe there is no `Jwt__Secret` override even though the gateway reads the secret from configuration.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docker-compose.yml` provides the gateway JWT secret via environment configuration.
- [ ] Gateway startup no longer relies on the placeholder secret in checked-in appsettings.
- [ ] End-to-end auth works with a non-default secret in local compose.

title: NatsEventSubscriber: unhandled exception in HandleAsync permanently kills subscriber
description:
In shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs (lines 21-27), the ConsumeAsync loop has zero error handling. If HandleAsync throws (e.g. deserialization error, business logic bug, transient DB failure), the exception propagates out of ExecuteAsync, terminating the BackgroundService. No more messages are ever processed for that consumer — it is permanently dead. BackgroundService silently swallows the exception (default .NET behavior), so there is no logging either. Fix: wrap HandleAsync + AckAsync in a try-catch with logging, retry logic, and a dead-letter/skip strategy for poison messages.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run a subscriber derived from `NatsEventSubscriber`.
2. Cause `HandleAsync` to throw for a consumed message.
3. Observe the background service stops processing messages instead of recovering safely.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Subscriber exceptions are logged and handled without permanently killing the consumer loop.
- [ ] Poison or transient failures follow a defined ack/nack/retry strategy.
- [ ] Tests cover the failure path so regressions are caught automatically.

title: Gateway has no test project — violates no-code-without-tests non-negotiable
description:
The API Gateway (services/gateway/) is the most complex service with real production logic: JWT authentication, YARP reverse proxy routing, rate limiting (two policies), CORS, InternalRouteBlockMiddleware, UserIdHeaderMiddleware, and aggregated health checks. Despite this, it has zero tests. No tests/ directory exists, no test project in Winzy.slnx (all other 6 services have test projects). This directly violates the project non-negotiable: No code without tests. Critical things to test: (1) InternalRouteBlockMiddleware blocks /habits/user/* and */internal/* with 403, (2) UserIdHeaderMiddleware strips spoofed X-User-Id and sets it from JWT claims, (3) YARP routes correctly match with auth policies, (4) rate limiting policies apply to correct routes.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Inspect `services/gateway/`.
2. Compare it with the other backend services.
3. Observe the gateway has no test project despite containing real middleware and routing logic.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The gateway has a dedicated test project in the solution.
- [ ] Core middleware/routing/auth behaviors are covered by automated tests.
- [ ] The gateway now satisfies the repository test non-negotiable.

title: Define cross-service habit visibility save orchestration
description:
The tracker currently states that Social owns visibility while Habit owns habits, but the create/edit/default-visibility flows still leave room for split-brain behavior. Decide and document the exact cross-service write/read contract before those flows are implemented broadly.

## Scope
- [ ] Define the create-habit flow when visibility must also be initialized or defaulted.
- [ ] Define the edit-habit flow when visibility changes alongside habit metadata changes.
- [ ] Decide the source of truth and fetch path for default visibility in create/edit/settings flows.
- [ ] Choose save ordering, idempotency expectations, and compensation/retry behavior when Habit and Social writes do not both succeed.
- [ ] Define the user-visible failure UX for partial success, retry, and recovery.

## Surfaces To Cover
- [ ] Habit create/edit flows (winzy.ai-3mk, winzy.ai-o84)
- [ ] Settings default-visibility preferences (winzy.ai-412)
- [ ] Public/share surfaces that depend on public visibility (winzy.ai-szy)
- [ ] Social backend ownership and contracts (winzy.ai-bdd)

## Notes
- No direct cross-service database access.
- This bead is about the contract and failure policy, not implementing every UI or endpoint itself.
- The outcome should preserve a low-friction habit flow without hiding correctness failures from users or operators.
acceptance_criteria: One explicit cross-service visibility write/read contract exists for create, edit, default-visibility, and public-sharing flows.\nPartial-success, retry, and user-facing failure behavior are documented clearly enough that Habit and Social implementations do not diverge.\nDependent beads reference this decision and inherit concrete verification/logging expectations instead of hand-waving the split across services.

title: Define privacy-sensitive invalidation strategy for visibility and friend changes
description:
Privacy-sensitive read models currently depend on Social visibility/friendship state, but the tracker does not yet make the invalidation path explicit. Decide and document how visibility changes and friend removal propagate to downstream consumers before those features land.

## Scope
- [ ] Decide whether downstream surfaces rely on synchronous Social re-checks on read, new NATS subjects/events, cache invalidation hooks, or a documented hybrid.
- [ ] Cover public flame reads, activity-feed eligibility, and any other visibility-sensitive derived data that should not stay stale after friend removal or visibility changes.
- [ ] Define acceptable staleness, if any, and the user/privacy boundary it must respect.
- [ ] Define the logging and verification needed to catch privacy regressions.

## Affected Areas
- [ ] Public Flame Page (winzy.ai-szy)
- [ ] Activity Service / feed (winzy.ai-ssb, winzy.ai-uut)
- [ ] Social visibility authority and event contracts (winzy.ai-bdd, shared subjects/contracts)

## Notes
- This is a contract/architecture decision bead, not a request to weaken privacy for caching convenience.
- If new events are required, the tracker should name them explicitly rather than leaving them implied.
acceptance_criteria: The tracker explicitly defines how friend-removal and visibility-change invalidation reaches public/share/feed consumers, including any new events or synchronous re-check rules.\nAcceptable staleness, privacy boundaries, and observability requirements are documented well enough to catch regressions in tests and during rollout.\nDependent beads no longer rely on implicit cache magic for privacy-sensitive data freshness.

title: Gateway base config trusts an overly broad 172.16.0.0/12 proxy range

title: Data export and account deletion
description:
Allow users to export their data and delete their account without manually dealing with individual services.

## Export Model
- [ ] One user-facing export action produces one documented bundled download through an orchestrated backend path (for example gateway- or auth-owned aggregation), not ad hoc client-side fan-out.
- [ ] Include the data owned by the in-scope services: Auth/profile data, habits/completions, friendships/visibility defaults, challenges, and any notification/activity data that remains in scope for this feature.
- [ ] JSON export is required; CSV is optional if it does not degrade fidelity.
- [ ] Download as file with clear completion/failure feedback.

## Delete My Account
- [ ] Confirmation dialog with warning
- [ ] Call Auth Service: DELETE /auth/account
- [ ] Auth Service publishes user.deleted event
- [ ] All in-scope services clean up their own user data on receiving the event
- [ ] Sign out and clear local session state
- [ ] Cannot be undone

## Orchestration And Verification
- [ ] Document the chosen export-bundle composition/orchestration model explicitly.
- [ ] Document how partial export or delayed deletion failures are surfaced and retried.
- [ ] Add end-to-end verification with strong correlation-friendly logging by user ID/request ID.

## Privacy
- [ ] Link to privacy policy in Settings
- [ ] Accessible from signup flow

## GDPR Considerations
- [ ] Right to access (export)
- [ ] Right to erasure (delete across all services)
- [ ] Data portability (export in common format)

## Notes
- Account deletion requires coordination across services via NATS.
- Export/delete should feel like one user action even though data ownership stays with the individual services.
acceptance_criteria: A documented orchestration model exists for single-action export and account deletion without leaking service topology into the UI.\nEach in-scope service has explicit export ownership and deletion-evidence expectations before the bundled workflow is implemented, including a clear inclusion/exclusion decision for derived notification/activity data.\nTests cover bundled export composition, auth-initiated deletion propagation, session invalidation after deletion, and diagnosable partial-failure paths with strong logs.

title: Define per-service export contracts for bundled account export and deletion evidence
description:

title: NATS Setup & Event Contracts
description:
Set up NATS messaging and define shared event contracts.

## NATS Server
- [ ] Add NATS container to docker-compose.yml
- [ ] Configure JetStream for persistent messaging — REQUIRED from day 1 (not optional)
- [ ] Expose management port for monitoring

## Shared Contracts (Winzy.Contracts project)
- [ ] Define C# record types for all events:
  - UserRegisteredEvent { Guid UserId, string Username }
  - UserDeletedEvent { Guid UserId }
  - HabitCreatedEvent { Guid UserId, Guid HabitId, string Name }
  - HabitCompletedEvent { Guid UserId, Guid HabitId, DateTime Date, double Consistency }
  - FriendRequestSentEvent { Guid FromUserId, Guid ToUserId }
  - FriendRequestAcceptedEvent { Guid UserId1, Guid UserId2 }
  - ChallengeCreatedEvent { Guid ChallengeId, Guid FromUser, Guid ToUser, Guid HabitId }
  - ChallengeCompletedEvent { Guid ChallengeId, Guid UserId, string Reward }

## NATS Client Helper (Winzy.Common project)
- [ ] NatsPublisher service for publishing events
- [ ] NatsSubscriber base class for consuming events
- [ ] JSON serialization configuration
- [ ] Connection string from environment variable
- [ ] Reconnection handling

## Subject Naming Convention
- user.registered
- user.deleted
- habit.created
- habit.completed
- friend.request.sent
- friend.request.accepted
- challenge.created
- challenge.completed

Note: No `events.` prefix — subjects match the event names directly. This aligns with KONZEPTDOKUMENT.md.

## Notes
- All services reference Winzy.Contracts for type-safe events
- Use NATS JetStream from day 1 — NOT core NATS. user.deleted must reach all 5 subscribers with at-least-once delivery guarantees. Core NATS is fire-and-forget: a service restarting during that event silently skips cleanup, causing orphaned data and GDPR violations.
- user.deleted triggers cascade cleanup in all services (Winzy.ai-7dh)

title: Backend Coding Guidelines
description:
Define .NET backend coding conventions for CLAUDE.md.

## Code Style
- [ ] .editorconfig with C# conventions
- [ ] PascalCase for public members, camelCase for locals
- [ ] File-scoped namespaces
- [ ] Primary constructors where appropriate

## Project Structure (per service)
- Program.cs — Service configuration and endpoint mapping
- Endpoints/ — Minimal API endpoint groups
- Entities/ — EF Core entity classes
- Services/ — Business logic
- Data/ — DbContext and migrations

## Patterns
- [ ] Minimal API endpoint groups (static classes with MapGroup)
- [ ] Dependency injection for all services
- [ ] Result pattern for error handling (no exceptions for business logic)
- [ ] Strongly-typed configuration (IOptions pattern)

## API Conventions
- [ ] REST resource naming (plural nouns)
- [ ] user_id from X-User-Id header (set by Gateway)
- [ ] Pagination with cursor-based approach

## Standard API Response Format
- [ ] Success: { data: T } or { data: T[], cursor: string }
- [ ] Error: { error: string, details?: string[] }
- [ ] HTTP status codes: 200 (OK), 201 (Created), 204 (No Content), 400 (Bad Request), 401 (Unauthorized), 404 (Not Found), 422 (Validation), 500 (Server Error)

## Cross-Service Response Contracts
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} returns: { data: [{ id, name, icon, color, frequency, consistency, visibility }] }
- [ ] GET /friends (Social → called by Activity Service): { data: [{ friendId }] }
- [ ] Document response shapes in shared/Winzy.Contracts/ alongside event types

## Testing Requirements (Non-Negotiable)
- [ ] Every endpoint: at least 1 integration test (Testcontainers + real PostgreSQL)
- [ ] Every business logic method: unit tests covering happy path + edge cases
- [ ] Every NATS handler: test that correct events trigger correct actions
- [ ] Coverage target: 80%+ per service
- [ ] Test naming: MethodName_Scenario_ExpectedResult

## Non-Negotiables
- [ ] No code merged without tests
- [ ] No direct DB access across service boundaries
- [ ] All inter-service communication via REST or NATS
- [ ] All endpoints require authentication except: /auth/register, /auth/login, /auth/refresh, /habits/public/{username}

## Notes
- These guidelines go into CLAUDE.md for AI-assisted development
- Keep it practical, not academic

title: Social Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Social Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /friends/request — Send friend request
- [ ] PUT /friends/request/{id}/accept — Accept request
- [ ] PUT /friends/request/{id}/decline — Decline request
- [ ] DELETE /friends/{id} — Remove friend
- [ ] GET /friends — List friends
- [ ] GET /friends/requests — List pending requests (incoming + outgoing)
- [ ] GET /friends/{id}/profile — Friend profile + visible habits
- [ ] PUT /friends/visibility/{habitId} — Set habit visibility
- [ ] GET/PUT social privacy preferences needed for default visibility on new habits

## Database Schema (social_db)
- friendships table: id, user_id, friend_id, status (pending/accepted), created_at
- visibility_settings table: id, user_id, habit_id, visibility (private/friends/public)
- social_preferences table (or equivalent): per-user defaults such as default habit visibility

## Cross-Service Communication
- [ ] Friend profile composition includes Auth-owned identity data (display name, avatar, member-since or equivalent public profile metadata).
- [ ] GET /friends/{id}/profile uses Habit Service habit data plus Social visibility rules.
- [ ] Social Service is the source of truth for who can see which habits and what the default sharing posture is.
- [ ] Public/profile read models that depend on visibility must consume Social's rules rather than duplicating their own source of truth.

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish friend.request.sent { fromUserId, toUserId }
- [ ] Publish friend.request.accepted { userId1, userId2 }
- [ ] Subscribe user.deleted → remove all friendships, visibility settings, and social preferences for userId

## Notes
- Friendship is bidirectional (both users see each other)
- Visibility defaults to private for new habits unless the user's explicit social preference says otherwise
- Social Service is the authority on who can see what

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria: Friendship, visibility, default-sharing preference, and profile-composition behavior work end-to-end with explicit internal-service and gateway-facing social route contracts.\nTests cover friendship state transitions, visibility enforcement, profile composition, user.deleted cleanup, and whichever invalidation strategy the tracker adopts for visibility/friend changes.\nStructured logs make social mutations, profile reads, privacy-sensitive cache/visibility paths, and failure reasons diagnosable by user IDs, relationship IDs, and visibility state.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 7 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] Friendship is bidirectional (both users see each other)
- [ ] Visibility defaults to private for new habits
- [ ] Cross-service call to Habit Service works (GET /habits/user/{userId})
- [ ] Habits filtered by visibility settings before returning
- [ ] friend.request.sent and friend.request.accepted events publish via NATS
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
- [ ] Integration tests for every endpoint (Testcontainers)
- [ ] Unit tests for visibility filtering logic
- [ ] 80%+ code coverage

title: Auth Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Auth Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /auth/register — Create account (hash password with Argon2id)
- [ ] POST /auth/login — Validate credentials, return JWT + refresh token
- [ ] POST /auth/refresh — Exchange refresh token for new JWT
- [ ] POST /auth/logout — Invalidate the current refresh token and clear the web refresh cookie/session bootstrap state
- [ ] GET /auth/profile — Get own profile
- [ ] PUT /auth/profile — Update name, avatar
- [ ] PUT /auth/password — Change password
- [ ] DELETE /auth/account — Delete account (publishes user.deleted event)
- [ ] GET /auth/users/search?q={query} — Search users by username/email (for friend discovery)

## Database Schema (auth_db)
- users table: id (UUID), username (unique, URL-safe, required), email, password_hash, display_name, avatar_url, created_at
- refresh_tokens table: id, user_id, token, expires_at, revoked_at

## JWT Configuration
- [ ] Access token: short-lived (15 min)
- [ ] Refresh token: long-lived (7 days)
- [ ] Sign with secret key (from environment variable)
- [ ] Include user_id and email in claims

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish user.registered { userId, username } on registration
- [ ] Publish user.deleted { userId } on account deletion

## Security
- [ ] Argon2id password hashing (replaces BCrypt — memory-hard, GPU/ASIC resistant, OWASP recommended for new projects)
- [ ] Validate email format
- [ ] Rate limiting on login attempts
- [ ] Refresh token rotation
- [ ] User search returns limited fields only (id, displayName, avatarUrl)

## Token Storage Strategy (Web PWA + Native)
- [ ] Access token: in-memory only (JS variable, 15 min TTL) — never written to storage, immune to XSS
- [ ] Refresh token (web): HttpOnly + SameSite=Strict cookie via Set-Cookie response header
- [ ] Refresh token (native): expo-secure-store (OK on native, XSS is not a concern there)
- [ ] On app load: call POST /auth/refresh to hydrate in-memory access token from cookie/store
- [ ] POST /auth/login response sets cookie on web AND returns refresh token in body for native
- [ ] POST /auth/refresh clears old cookie and sets new one (rotation)
- [ ] POST /auth/logout clears HttpOnly cookie server-side and invalidates the corresponding refresh token/session

**Why:** expo-secure-store silently falls back to localStorage on web, making the refresh token XSS-stealable. HttpOnly cookies cannot be read by JS at all.

## Notes
- Profile data (name, avatar) also lives here
- Other services identify users by user_id from JWT (injected by Gateway)
- Password reset flow (forgot password → email → reset) deferred to post-MVP
acceptance_criteria:
Registration, login, refresh, logout, profile, and user-search behaviors work end-to-end with the intended web/native session model.
Tests cover password hashing, username validation/collision handling, refresh rotation, logout cookie/token invalidation, and account-deletion event publication.
Logs and failure diagnostics make auth/session failures understandable without exposing secrets, raw credentials, or token material.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 9 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] JWT generation and validation works (access + refresh tokens)
- [ ] Logout invalidates the relevant refresh token/session and clears the web cookie path correctly
- [ ] Argon2id password hashing implemented
- [ ] user.registered event publishes via NATS
- [ ] user.deleted event publishes via NATS
- [ ] User search returns limited fields only
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
- [ ] Integration tests for every endpoint (Testcontainers)
- [ ] Unit tests for JWT generation, password hashing, validation, and logout/session invalidation rules
- [ ] 80%+ code coverage

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Switched from BCrypt to Argon2id for password hashing. OWASP recommends Argon2id for new projects — it's memory-hard and resistant to GPU/ASIC attacks. .NET package: Konscious.Security.Cryptography.Argon2 or Isopoh.Cryptography.Argon2.

title: Docker Compose health checks and service boot order
description:
Prevent services from crashing on startup because PostgreSQL or NATS isn't ready yet.

## Problem
Without health checks and `depends_on: condition: service_healthy`, .NET services try to
connect to their database the moment Docker starts them — before PostgreSQL has finished
initialising. This causes startup crashes and requires manual `docker compose restart`.

## PostgreSQL Health Checks
Add to every Postgres container in docker-compose.yml:
```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres -d ${DB_NAME}"]
  interval: 5s
  timeout: 5s
  retries: 10
  start_period: 10s
```

## NATS Health Check
```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:8222/healthz || exit 1"]
  interval: 5s
  timeout: 5s
  retries: 10
```

## Service Boot Order
Every .NET service should declare:
```yaml
depends_on:
  {service}-db:
    condition: service_healthy
  nats:
    condition: service_healthy
```

## Checklist
- [ ] healthcheck added to all 6 PostgreSQL containers (auth-db, habit-db, social-db, challenge-db, notification-db, activity-db)
- [ ] healthcheck added to NATS container

title: Error handling and offline state management
description:
Define the shared frontend runtime behavior for errors, offline state, retries, and session recovery. This issue should keep major screens from inventing their own token-refresh and error-handling logic.

## Shared API/Session Layer
- [ ] Centralized API client with consistent request/response/error handling.
- [ ] Session bootstrap on app load using the supported auth refresh flow.
- [ ] Queue/retry behavior for requests interrupted by token refresh.
- [ ] Timeout and retry handling for transient failures.

## Error And Offline UX
- [ ] Friendly, actionable error messaging that aligns with the product tone.
- [ ] Shared loading/empty/error presentation patterns for major screens.
- [ ] Offline detection, subtle offline indicators, and graceful disabling/retry behavior.
- [ ] React error boundaries and safe fallback UI for unexpected client crashes.

## Logging And Diagnostics
- [ ] Structured client-side logging for token-refresh failures, retried requests, surfaced API errors, and offline/online transitions during E2E/debug runs.
- [ ] Document how downstream screens should hook into the shared runtime instead of bypassing it.

## Notes
- Server remains the source of truth; this is graceful recovery, not offline-first sync.
- The goal is consistency and recoverability across screens.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Major frontend flows can rely on one shared session/error/offline runtime.
Tests cover refresh/retry behavior, offline transitions, and user-facing error presentation.
Client-side diagnostics are detailed enough to explain failed auth/network flows during debugging and E2E runs.
close_reason: Frontend error handling with ErrorBoundary and OfflineIndicator implemented

title: Consistency calculation algorithm (60-day rolling window)
description:
Define and implement the consistency calculation that powers The Flame.

## Core Algorithm
- [ ] Calculate: (actual completions / expected completions) over rolling window
- [ ] Default window: 60 days
- [ ] Result: percentage 0-100

## Edge Cases
- [ ] New habits (<60 days): Use available days as denominator
- [ ] Habits started today: Show 0% or 'Just started' state
- [ ] Weekly habits: Expected = weeks in window, not days
- [ ] Custom frequency: Calculate expected based on selected days

## The Flame Mapping
- [ ] Define percentage to Flame intensity mapping
- [ ] 'Grows quickly': Fast response to improvement
- [ ] 'Shrinks slowly': Gradual decline, forgiving

## Example Calculations
Daily habit, 60-day window:
- 60/60 completions = 100% (blazing)
- 45/60 completions = 75% (strong)
- 30/60 completions = 50% (medium)
- 6/60 completions = 10% (ember)

Weekly habit (1x per week), 60-day window (~8 weeks):
- 8/8 completions = 100%
- 6/8 completions = 75%

## Implementation
- [ ] Backend: calculateConsistency() in Habit Service (.NET)
- [ ] Returned as part of GET /habits/{id}/stats response
- [ ] Frontend: getFlameIntensity(consistencyPercentage) for visual mapping
- [ ] Consider caching with smart invalidation


## Timezone Handling (CRITICAL)
- [ ] Consistency window uses the user's LOCAL calendar dates, not UTC
- [ ] '60-day window start' = midnight of (today - 60 days) in user's timezone
- [ ] Client must supply IANA timezone (e.g. America/New_York) with every completion
- [ ] Stored as local_date column on the completions table for fast window queries
- Edge case: user logs at 11 PM UTC-5 → server sees 4 AM next day UTC → must use local_date, not completed_at

## Notes
- This replaces streaks as the PRIMARY metric
- Implemented in the Habit Service backend, consumed by frontend
- No streak counters anywhere in the app — consistency is the only metric

(Originally GH RewardStack #72)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:
The consistency algorithm defines one clear backend/frontend contract, including the response fields needed by flame and stats consumers.
Tests cover boundary math, timezone-sensitive windows, and representative daily/weekly/custom-frequency scenarios.
The frontend-facing flame/intensity mapping inputs are explicit enough that downstream components do not reinterpret the rules differently.
close_reason: Flame intensity mapping with asymmetric thresholds and 54 new tests

title: EF Core & PostgreSQL Migrations
description:
Establish the shared EF Core + PostgreSQL migration conventions and bootstrapping pattern used by all services. This issue owns the common approach; concrete service schemas still live in the relevant service implementation beads.

## Shared EF/Migration Foundation
- [ ] Standardize package choices, DbContext registration, migration project layout, and naming conventions.
- [ ] Define shared PostgreSQL conventions: snake_case naming, UUID keys, timestamptz/date usage, and indexing expectations.
- [ ] Document the migration/bootstrapping pattern every service follows in development and CI.

## Runtime Behavior
- [ ] Services can apply pending migrations safely in the supported development workflow.
- [ ] Migration startup logs clearly show what was applied or why startup failed.
- [ ] Migration application is idempotent for a clean database and a partially initialized database.

## Validation
- [ ] At least one representative service proves the pattern end-to-end with integration coverage.
- [ ] Service-specific beads remain responsible for their own entities, schema details, and concrete migrations.
- [ ] Dev seed-data behavior is kept separate from this issue unless it is needed only to validate the migration pattern.

## Notes
- This issue should reduce duplicated EF setup work without hiding service-specific schema ownership.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
A documented migration pattern exists that service beads can reuse consistently.
Clean and partially initialized databases can boot through the supported migration path with useful logs.
The issue no longer ambiguously owns every service schema in full.

title: Setup testing infrastructure
description:
Configure testing infrastructure for frontend and backend. Testing is a hard gate: implementation beads should be able to rely on a real shared harness instead of inventing ad-hoc tests per feature.

## Testing Philosophy
- Every backend service gets unit tests for domain logic and validators.
- Every backend HTTP surface gets integration tests against real infrastructure.
- Messaging behavior is covered with JetStream-enabled integration tests, not only mocks.
- Frontend user flows get component/hook coverage plus Playwright journeys for critical paths.
- Failures must be diagnosable from logs and artifacts, not just a red test name.

## Frontend Testing Foundation
- [ ] Configure Jest for the Expo web-first app with shared render/test utilities.
- [ ] Provide patterns for component, hook, and screen tests.
- [ ] Standardize API, storage, and navigation mocking helpers.
- [ ] Add coverage reporting and root-level commands for local and CI runs.

## Backend Testing Foundation
- [ ] Create reusable xUnit test patterns for all services.
- [ ] Provide Testcontainers helpers for PostgreSQL and JetStream-enabled NATS.
- [ ] Add reusable helpers for HTTP assertions, DB assertions, and event assertions.
- [ ] Cover startup stream setup, publish/subscribe flows, malformed payload handling, retry/ack behavior, and dependency failure scenarios.
- [ ] Avoid the known concurrent `dotnet test` file-locking problem by serializing runs or isolating outputs when needed.

## Diagnostics And Artifacts
- [ ] Test failures emit useful logs: request/response details, affected entity IDs, emitted NATS subjects, and relevant assertion context.
- [ ] CI publishes machine-readable test results plus human-useful failure artifacts.
- [ ] Document how feature beads are expected to add or extend tests using the shared harness.

## CI Integration
- [ ] Frontend and backend test commands run from the repo root.
- [ ] Coverage/results are visible in CI checks.
- [ ] Later feature and bug beads can depend on this issue as the canonical test foundation.

## Notes
- Playwright runner/setup is tracked in winzy.ai-ftb.
- Project-scoped agent MCP config is tracked separately in winzy.ai-dy7.
- Better to tighten the harness now than rediscover infrastructure bugs during feature work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
A shared frontend/backend test harness exists with documented local and CI entry points.
Backend integration coverage uses real PostgreSQL and JetStream-enabled NATS containers rather than mock-only messaging tests.
Failure output includes actionable logs/artifacts so feature teams can debug broken flows quickly.

title: Local development data seeding script
description:
Create a local development seed flow that populates the running stack with realistic but deterministic data for cross-service testing and demos.

## Seed Goals
- [ ] Create a stable set of users, habits, completions, friendships, visibility settings, and at least one challenge.
- [ ] Use deterministic identities, usernames, and passwords so docs and tests can reference them consistently.
- [ ] Produce data varied enough to exercise different flame states and social scenarios.

## Execution Model
- [ ] Prefer seeding through the API Gateway so the real request path is exercised.
- [ ] Decide whether the script is idempotent, resettable, or both; document that choice clearly.
- [ ] Make the minimum required stack explicit before the seed flow runs.

## Scope Boundary
- [ ] Core deterministic coverage is required for Auth, Habit, Social, and Challenge domains.
- [ ] If derived consumers such as Notification or Activity are also seeded/verified, that must be explicit and deterministic rather than implied.
- [ ] If derived consumers are out of scope for the current seed flow, the output/docs must say so clearly to avoid misleading demos.

## Output And Diagnostics
- [ ] Print a verbose summary of created or reused entities, including stable IDs/usernames where helpful.
- [ ] Make failures easy to diagnose with step-by-step logging rather than a generic crash.
- [ ] Document how the seed data maps to common manual test and demo scenarios.

## Notes
- This is for local development and demos, not production bootstrapping.
- Keep the data realistic, but favor determinism over randomness.
acceptance_criteria:
A documented seed flow can populate deterministic core domain data across the supported local stack.
The seed output clearly states whether derived consumers such as notifications/activity were also verified or are intentionally out of scope for that run.
Manual testing and demos can rely on known seeded users/relationships, and failures are diagnosable from the script output.

title: Observability: structured logging and request correlation
description:
Add structured HTTP/service logging and request correlation across the backend. This issue is about making request flows and background event handling diagnosable, not about redefining `/health` semantics.

## Scope

- [ ] Ensure HTTP logs consistently include route, method, status, latency, and relevant entity/user identifiers when safe.
- [ ] Ensure NATS/background-handler logs include subject, consumer/subscriber identity, message/correlation IDs, retry outcome, and failure reason.
- [ ] Document local and CI log expectations so failing tests and demos are easier to diagnose.

## Non-Goals
- [ ] `/health` payload truthfulness belongs to winzy.ai-2m3.
- [ ] Larger event-trace propagation changes should be tracked separately if they become necessary.

## Notes

- Keep personally sensitive data out of logs.
acceptance_criteria:
Gateway and backend services emit structured HTTP and background-handler logs with usable request/correlation context.
Main request and event-processing paths can be followed across gateway, service, and consumer boundaries during debugging and failing tests.
The issue stays narrowly focused on observability/correlation rather than absorbing unrelated health-check or user-copy concerns.
close_reason: Structured logging and correlation ID propagation added across all services

title: Service health wiring is inconsistent: auth requires INatsConnection that is never registered
description: auth-service adds AddNatsHealthCheck() but no service registers AddNatsMessaging(), so /health will attempt to construct NatsHealthCheck without an INatsConnection. At the same time, the other services still expose bare health checks without DB/NATS probes, so the gateway's aggregate health remains misleading. Fix service startup wiring and align all services with the documented health contract.
acceptance_criteria: Backend services either register NATS messaging before NATS health probes or intentionally omit those probes where NATS is not in scope, and /health wiring is consistent with the documented contract.\nTests cover service startup, per-service health responses, and at least one aggregated-health path with realistic dependency wiring.\nLogs make missing INatsConnection registration or broken dependency probes obvious instead of failing mysteriously.

description:

- [ ] Add pre-push hook

- [ ] Backend: Run dotnet format --verify-no-changes on staged .cs files

- [ ] Backend: dotnet build + dotnet test on changed service projects
- [ ] Fail push if any test fails

## Notes

- Pre-push = full test suite (catches bugs before they reach remote)
- No code reaches the remote without passing tests

title: Activity Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Activity Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints

## Activity Record Model (activity_db)

## NATS Subscriptions

- [ ] habit.created → create a structured new-habit activity event

title: Habit Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Habit Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /habits — Create new habit
- [ ] GET /habits — List own habits
- [ ] GET /habits/{id} — Habit details
- [ ] PUT /habits/{id} — Update habit
- [ ] DELETE /habits/{id} — Delete/archive habit
- [ ] POST /habits/{id}/complete — Mark habit complete (body: { date?: string, timezone: string } — timezone REQUIRED to resolve 'today' correctly for the user's locale)
- [ ] DELETE /habits/{id}/completions/{date} — Remove a completion (for corrections via calendar view)
- [ ] GET /habits/{id}/stats — Consistency stats (60-day rolling window)
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} — Internal: habits for a user (called by Social Service)
- [ ] GET /habits/public/{username} — Public flame data for shareable profiles, backed by exact username resolution plus visibility-aware read logic

## Database Schema (habit_db)
- habits table: id, user_id, name, icon, color, frequency, custom_days, created_at, archived_at
- completions table: id, habit_id, user_id, completed_at, local_date, note

## Consistency Algorithm
- [ ] 60-day rolling window calculation
- [ ] Handle edge cases: new habits, weekly frequency, custom days
- [ ] Return percentage 0-100
- [ ] All window boundary calculations use the user's supplied IANA timezone

## Timezone Handling
- completions.completed_at stored as timestamptz (UTC) in DB
- completions.local_date stores the user's local calendar date when the completion was logged
- Backend derives 'today' and 60-day window start from the user's supplied timezone, not server UTC
- Prevents: user in UTC-5 logging at 10 PM being counted as the next day

## Cross-Service Responsibilities
- [ ] Habit Service owns habit data and consistency calculations.
- [ ] Social Service owns visibility rules and privacy defaults; Habit Service must not become the source of truth for visibility state.
- [ ] Public flame reads can use an internal composition/projection, but they must respect Auth username identity and Social visibility rules.

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish habit.created { userId, habitId, name }
- [ ] Publish habit.completed { userId, habitId, date, consistency }
- [ ] Subscribe user.deleted → delete all habits and completions for userId

## Authorization
- [ ] user_id from X-User-Id header (set by Gateway)
- [ ] Users can only access their own habits
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} is internal-only (no auth check, trusted network)

## Notes
- Core service of the application
- Consistency calculation is the heart of the product (replaces streaks)

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Shared NATS subscriber consumes entire wildcard streams instead of filtering to one subject
description:
`shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/JetStreamSetup.cs` creates broad streams (`user.>`, `habit.>`, `friend.>`, `challenge.>`), while `shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs` creates consumers without configuring `FilterSubject`/`FilterSubjects`. JetStream therefore delivers every subject in that stream to the consumer. A subscriber intended for `user.deleted` on the `USERS` stream would also receive `user.registered`; a `habit.completed` subscriber on `HABITS` would also receive `habit.created`.

This is not only a routing bug. It sits in the same subscriber surface area that previously needed resilience fixes, so the final plan must ensure wrong-subject or incompatible payloads are handled safely and logged clearly rather than killing the consumer loop.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Create a subscriber derived from `NatsEventSubscriber<T>` for one event type on a wildcard stream such as `USERS`.
2. Publish a different event on another subject within the same stream (for example `user.registered`).
3. Observe the consumer still receives that message because no subject filter was configured.

## Notes
- This blocks every service that relies on subject-specific JetStream consumers.
- The fix should verify both routing correctness and failure isolation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
`NatsEventSubscriber` requires/configures an explicit subject filter for the intended event subject(s).
Wrong-subject or incompatible payload deliveries are logged and isolated safely rather than killing the subscriber loop.
At least one real JetStream integration test proves a consumer such as `user.deleted` ignores `user.registered` while the consumer stays healthy.

title: Challenge Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Challenge Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /challenges — Create challenge for a friend
- [ ] GET /challenges — List own challenges (sent + received)
- [ ] GET /challenges/{id} — Challenge details + progress
- [ ] PUT /challenges/{id}/claim — Mark reward as claimed
- [ ] DELETE /challenges/{id} — Cancel challenge

## Database Schema (challenge_db)
- challenges table: id, habit_id, creator_id, recipient_id, milestone_type, target_value, period_days, reward_description, status, created_at, ends_at, completed_at

## NATS Subscriptions
- [ ] Subscribe to habit.completed
- [ ] Subscribe to user.deleted → cancel all challenges involving userId
- [ ] On event: find active challenges for this habit_id
- [ ] Calculate progress toward milestone
- [ ] If milestone reached: set status=completed, publish challenge.completed

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish challenge.created { challengeId, fromUser, toUser, habitId }
- [ ] Publish challenge.completed { challengeId, userId, reward }

## Notes
- Rewards are shared experiences (coffee, hiking) — NOT material gifts
- No failed state — challenges expire gracefully
- Challenge creator must be a friend of the recipient (validate via Social Service)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:

Tests cover milestone evaluation, event-handler idempotency, safe retry/poison-message behavior, and challenge completion edge cases.
Structured logs make challenge command handling and habit-event processing diagnosable by subject, challenge ID, habit ID, and failure reason.
